items.name: Classification of Integer Pairs
Bounded Set, Upper/Lower Bound, Least Upper/Greatest Lower Bound (Rationals)
items.statement:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{Q}$.
(i) The set $A$ is \textbf{bounded} if there exists $M \in \mathbb{Q}_{>0}$ such that $|q| \leq M$ for all $q \in A$.
(ii) A \textbf{lower bound} for $A$ is a number $\ell \in \mathbb{Q}$ such that $\ell \leq q$ for all $q \in A$.
(iii) An \textbf{upper bound} for $A$ is a number $u \in \mathbb{Q}$ such that $q \leq u$ for all $q \in A$.
(iv) A number $\ell \in \mathbb{Q}$ is a \textbf{greatest lower bound} for $A$ if $\ell' \leq \ell$ for every lower bound $\ell'$ for $A$.
(v) A number $u \in \mathbb{Q}$ is a \textbf{least upper bound} for $A$ if $u \leq u'$ for every upper bound $u'$ for $A$.

items.name: Cauchy Sequence and Convergence (Rationals)
items.statement:
Let $(q_n)$ be a sequence in $\mathbb{Q}$.
(i) The sequence $(q_n)$ is a \textbf{Cauchy sequence} if, for every $\epsilon \in \mathbb{Q}_{>0}$, there exists $N \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ such that $|q_n - q_m| < \epsilon$ for $m, n \geq N$.
(ii) The sequence $(q_n)$ \textbf{converges} to $L \in \mathbb{Q}$ if, for every $\epsilon \in \mathbb{Q}_{>0}$, there exists $N \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ such that $|q_n - L| < \epsilon$ for $n \geq N$.

items.name: Bounded Below, Bounded Above, Bounded, Unbounded (Reals)
items.statement: A nonempty subset $A$ of $\mathbb{R}$ is said to be \textbf{bounded below} if there is a number $s$ such that $s \leq a$ for all $a \in A$ and in this case any such number $s$ is called a \textbf{lower bound} for $A$. Similarly, $A$ is said to be \textbf{bounded above} if there is a number $t$ such that $a \leq t$ for all $a \in A$, and any such number $t$ is called an \textbf{upper bound} for $A$. Finally, $A$ is said to be \textbf{bounded} if it is both bounded below and bounded above and to be \textbf{unbounded} if it is not bounded.

items.name: Least Upper Bound Property
items.statement: Every nonempty subset of $\mathbb{R}$ that is bounded above has a least upper bound, i.e., an upper bound that is smaller than any other upper bound. Similarly, every nonempty subset of $\mathbb{R}$ that is bounded below has a greatest lower bound, i.e., a lower bound that is greater than any other lower bound.

items.name: Supremum and Infimum
items.statement:
Suppose that $A$ is a nonempty subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
(i) If $A$ is bounded above then the smallest upper bound for $A$ is called the \textbf{least upper bound} or \textbf{supremum} of $A$ and is denoted by $\sup A$.
(ii) If $A$ is bounded below then the largest lower bound for $A$ is called the \textbf{greatest lower bound} or \textbf{infimum} of $A$ and is denoted by $\inf A$.

items.name: Monotonicity of Supremum
items.statement: If $A$ and $B$ are two nonempty subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ that are bounded above and if $A \subseteq B$ then $\sup A \leq \sup B$.

items.name: Characterization of Supremum
items.statement:
If $A$ is a nonempty subset of $\mathbb{R}$ that is bounded above and if $s \in \mathbb{R}$ is an upper bound for $A$, then $s = \sup A$ if and only if
(i) $a \leq s$ for all $a \in A$ and
(ii) $A \cap (s - \epsilon, s] \neq \emptyset$ for all $\epsilon > 0$.
If $A$ is a nonempty subset of $\mathbb{R}$ that is bounded above and if $s = \sup A$ then either $s \in A$ or else $A \cap (s - \epsilon, s) \neq \emptyset$ for all $\epsilon > 0$.
